title: Remote Control Car
subtitle: ASME * RC Derby 2026
blurb: Remote controlled car built using custom 3D printed parts and woodcut scraps. Assisted in development of steering system and overall chassis construction.
description: This project was done for the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) student chapter at the University of Central Florida, specifically for their RC Derby, a competition tasking teams with building a remote-controlled car under size, battery, and component restrictions, then racing it around a small track for time.

The initial design consisted almost entirely of 3D-printed parts for ease of construction and weight. The components would rest on a flat plate, with the electronics (battery, ESC, microcontroller / receiver) in the center, motors in the rear, and a servo-steering system in the front. The steering system was originally a complicated mechanism involving several shafts and gears, along with other concepts that we experimented with. We eventually landed on the steering 'rod' design, where a servo pushes a rigid arm left or right to then rotate a set of wheels in that direction. The motors and electrical components remained unchanged, and did not need much innovation.

Overall, the car was serviceable for how quickly it came together. Since most of the legwork was refining the steering, and considering we all had little experience with engineering as a whole during this project, I'm glad it worked well enough on race day. Still, the car was not without its issues — the joints that the wheels rested on had some difficulty turning smoothly, and our turning radius suffered as a result. The front wheels often slid instead of rolling, which hurt our speed. Nevertheless, we completed the loop several times and finished with a time of [TIME]. While we could have done better on race day, I am more satisfied with my growth as a result of this project. I became more comfortable with OnShape and the engineering design process, specifically in attempting to model as much as I could on my own. While it was difficult to model the wheel mounts and pins to be the perfect size, the trial-and-error was key to my learning. My teammates helped me better understand soldering and the order in which the electronics operated. Most importantly, this project pushed me to be a more effective leader, as I would often close group meetings with reminders, deadlines, and task assignments for each member.

images.caption: The completed car with all its systems in place.
From left to right, Matthew Julian González, Philip Pereira, Rogelio Padron Hernandez, Raul Crisppi, Gus.
First sketch of the car after meeting a handful of times.
Advice from ASME's project lead Will on how to properly solder the motor to the ESC.
Advice from ASME's project lead Will on how the servo works.
Mockup on custom 3D-printed pieces to mount the servo to the car's base.
A more finalized version of the car's steering system. The final steering system closely resembles this mockup.
Various pins and collars for different uses across the car.

title: Autonomous Submarine
subtitle: Intro to Engineering * Great Naval Orange Race 2026
blurb: Fully autonomous submarine built using custom 3D printed parts alongside readymade RC components. Assisted in propulsion systems and hull design.
description: Paragraph 1 * introduce the project and the problem it was solving.
Paragraph 2 * walk through your specific contribution and design decisions.
Paragraph 3 * results, what you learned, what you'd do differently.

images.caption: From left to right, Tanish Pilli, Felix Lee, Robert Hagerty (EGS Lab Team Leader), Matthew Julian González, Jalol Nazirov. This picture was taken on Great Navel Orange Race 2026 Race Day.

First rough sketch showing the various planned components in little detail. In this original design, a rudder system and ballast system were proposed, both of which did not make it to the final build.
A more refined version of the initial sketch.
The original proposed rudder system. The later designs were offshoots of this concept.

The first proposed measurements for the diameter and length of the cylindrical component of the hull. These measurements ended up being much larger than we could account for and had to be changed, thus the title 'Blind' Measurements.
The original submarine design, featuring the old rudder shroud and 'door' concepts scaled down to the size of the palm of ones hand.
Working into the wee hours before race day!

images.src: https://media.matthewjgonzalez.me/gnor-submarine/Later Rudder Control System and Rudder Sketches.jpeg
images.caption: A later proposed idea for a rudder control system and 3D printed rudders.
A later steering system with measurements and contours more accurate to the submarine's hull design at the time.
